sentences.text: Well, we departed from-- from Halifax.
And took us about five, five and a half days-- five and 3/4 day when we landed in Glasgow, in-- in-- in Scotland.
That was a-- an experience.
One funny thing-- very funny thing that has happened.
There was a Scottish guy in-- in my neighborhood, and we got-- we got friendly on-- on the ship.
And when we landed there-- and the Scottish language-- it's so-- real Scottish to-- to say-- so-- and he was Scottish himself.
And he elbowed me, hey, what-- what they are talking about?
And I told him well, so-- he's-- your grandfather is talking.
You don't understand your-- you're asking me, the green one?
This kind of episode, you see, it's never-- you can never forget.
But anyway, we disembark-- disembarked right-- right into theatre.
The troop train was waiting for us.
We ride right through the night.
It was sometimes 9 o'clock in the morning when arrived in-- in London.
And there, they were waiting for us.
And the group were always, always an old guest group, the nine of us.
But those who were awaiting us, waiting for us, they knew.
And they already had a place prepared and took us-- waiting us, and took us into the Russell Square, I think the name-- Russel Square into the Myers hotel.
And they put up us in a hotel.
You never stayed in the army barracks, no.
We were always-- always in special-- what do you call it?
Isolated from-- from everybody, and because of-- of the nature of the service were were in it already.
And there-- from there, we were taken out-- we were taken out to several places.
The British government or the military authorities were-- they expropriated this-- the English countryside, you know.
They had this-- this lordly-- lordly castles and so on.
There were several of them then-- then for the duration.
And they converted them into-- into training schools, you see?
And we had most our-- most of intelligent training, you see?
And the funniest thing that-- that we had on the-- on the real intelligence work, you see, how-- how-- how spies work, we had to know some-- something of it, because we had to make-- make contacts.
And our instructor was Kim Philby.
[LAUGHS]
We didn't know it at the time.
And all the- all the time that I found out years-- many years later, you know, when he was exposed as a-- as a so-called Soviet spy.
And see-- see his picture in-- in and look and look and turn away and looked again.
Well, he's dumb, and sure enough there a doubt to be that-- that he was-- he was-- well, he was in full confidence at the-- at the time.
And did you trust him as a-- as a real master spy, you see?
To give-- Did he-- did he give you his name?
Did you know his name?
No, no.
My dear, as soon as-- as soon as-- back here, it was all right.
But as soon as we landed in-- landed in England, well we had to-- had to strip our-- our real names.
And we-- and I had about six names by the time I got through this whole-- whole-- whole business.

And then, he-- he told us-- but not-- not in a class, but in the-- how much he talked to others, but for the two of us who he himself picked, well, these are the two-- two-- two-- the two-- two-- top ones.

And so he-- just in the coffee break or the lunch break-- told-- told us that-- how to get in touch with the hard to find communist movement in-- in-- in fascist countries, in bad countries, because he knew that they were the best country-- and they never made-- any-- what you call it-- any secret of it.
But in-- in a lecture room, he couldn't come out open, but he told us personally, like in Hungary, because there, the communist were really in-- behind the skirts of Social Democratic Party.
They were actually built in, you see?
That-- that-- that's how they operated.
And-- and then he told us that this kinds of tricks, you see-- how-- how to find him.
And so-- and sure enough, when we landed-- I landed in-- in the Hungary then-- and the people, my-- my contacts-- my first contacts were-- were Social Democrats.
And so they already had contact with the-- the British Labor Party through the British embassy in-- in Hungary.
Well then-- then-- we were-- we were trained in-- in England before we go-- before we go any further-- so this intelligence.
Courses were-- were-- were one after the other.
The-- the-- the-- the most amazing things that they did they taught us.
And just, we inject when I came back from the war.
And so I had to be careful for about two years.
I was my own-- own-- own-- own prisoner.
I was my own prisoner, because I-- I was tempted-- I was tempted to do things that it was absolutely out.
Look, I could open and safes and so on.
So we were trained so better-- the chief-- [LAUGHS] three-- they had three safe crackers in-- in England who were in jail.
And they-- they were able to make deal with one of them to be an instructor.
And they reduced his-- his sentence considerably.
They-- they brought in peoples like-- like them to-- to-- to train us, you see?
And they have to-- So we were fully trained in about-- when we came back, we had to forget 90%-- 90% of it, just because this all dirty tricks in-- in that kind of operation.
And then we had our parachute training there in Manchester, about nine-- nine miles.
I-- I keep forgetting.
I discard things, all in a book, the place where we had our parachute training and-- and our-- what went on.
Some-- some pleasant things, like my incident with Merline who jumped in the-- in one of the night-- night-- night jumps.
She-- she jumped ahead of me, and she landed on me really-- really funny, because it was real-- real dark you see.
And there was no question of dark or no dark or fog in-- in England.
A time came you just had to-- had to jump and make-- make our practice jumps, because you never know, in-- in an operation, what kind of night you gonna-- you gonna hit.
So and many times, they selected precisely, intentionally, the toughest situation, the tough situation and places and so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to-- to train for this kind of things.
After Manchester, where did you go?

Well, and then-- well, after the parachute-- parachute jump, because it was something, you see, that-- that really excited you, and-- and they-- they-- they made-- made you a nervous wreck and so on, because that-- that event was so specialized, not like the paratroops and so on, but it was specialized.
From Manchester, we were shipped out to-- well, we were shipped not-- not out yet from England, but we had about 10 days-- 10 days resting place in-- in Southampton-- Northampton-- Northampton.
And there, we found out several things.
And one was that the first trade union movement started in Northampton with the leatherworkers, shoemakers, and tanner-- tanner worker, and so on.
And then we-- we made big friends with ATS girls, which was the woman's army girls.
They had a camp there, near there.
And we went into town, into the pub and-- and so on.
And we were trained up in-- up in Scotland, in the rough land.
And part of it-- part of it was like the lower Carpathians, you see?
It was very, very, very, very, very good training there, but it was real tough, you know?
It's was rocks there.
The only pleasant things were the Scottish lassies.
[LAUGHS] When-- when did you end up in Cairo?
In Cairo?
Well, we landed-- landed there in the first week in January, 1944.
Because those who went to Cairo was-- some, like the Yugoslavs, or most of the Yugoslavs were parachuted in from Algiers.
Well, that-- you know, that the war was still-- still on partly in the-- the-- the North-- North African battle there.
And they weren't ready for-- for this operation after they defeated the Germans there.
But by the time we were shipped-- shipped over there, well, we were taken-- taken into-- into here, Cairo.
And-- and-- and from there, well--
Well, in Cairo, well, really, really not much.
We attended a few-- a few classes there, just to-- just to-- to-- to freshen up and smooth up, and so on.
And most of the time we waited.
Read They had all the radios.
And so we waited for the news from Hungary, what is happening in Hungary, and around Hungary, and in the whole-- whole-- whole-- operational field, how the war is going.
You see that, or the battle, that was on-- went on in-- in Russia, dominance in the Soviet territories.
And Hungary was-- Hungary was in it.
And it was hot stuff.
Well, the country next to Germany, it was Hungary.
Because Hungary was fascist right, from 1919.
Well, we were placed, the two of us-- our group-- oh, by the way, our group-- no, there were only-- only-- only eight of us.
Because one failed at the other training in the camp, actually, so that there was eight of us.
But only-- only two of us were shipped from-- from England to-- to-- to Cairo.
And there was one-- there was one who-- who dropped in from-- from-- from-- oh jeez, but we weren't sure.
He just disappeared.
He was taken-- taken away just before Christmas, from England.
And they told us that he disappeared some-- somewhere from over in Europe.
And we had news only years later that he-- he-- he disappeared in-- over the Atlantic.
And somebody saw him in Yugoslavia.
I met the Yugoslav Partisan comrade some 20 years back, in-- in Toronto.
He said, well, he was-- he was with us there, [INAUDIBLE].
Well, we are three different places already.
So that's how it happened, to-- to some of the pieces.
They just disappeared and goodbye for.
Well-- Well, we were then put into-- into a private house, just the two of us.
We had a-- we had an Arab-- an Egyptian Arab as a cook.
And well, he-- he looked after-- the housekeeper looked after us.
And-- and about two weeks-- two weeks later-- later, we found out right from him that he knew some Hungarian.
And how that-- that we found that out, but he used to work with the Hungarian embassy at-- at one time.
And they learned some Hungarian.
That's why they put-- put-- on this.
But everything was-- was really planned.
There was an army barrack for the British, you see, and we went in for-- went in for-- for-- for the intelligence training mostly, as they hear the news.
You know, it was everything, but all right, you have to watch this and then know.
So-- and--
And-- well, this one.
And I think it was in the third or four days, we went out with Adam-- Adam was his name, my-- my-- my partner, my Canadian partner-- Muhammed Ali Boulevard, we went out for-- for a-- for a stroll.
There was nothing else to do.
We didn't even know where we are going.
But we were just going into further-- into the city and towards the railway station.
And on the Main Street there, we come to-- come to a point where we heard Hungarian voices.
There were four-- four boys and one girl.
And-- [LAUGHS] We come closer.
There are children of Hungarian.
And then they were really sharply discussing something.
And he comes a few steps closer.
They started talking about Morse and Langer and Stalin and so on.
And so-- so we just came right up to them and greeted in-- greeted them in Hungarian.
[HUNGARIAN] [LAUGHS] Oh, it was so [INAUDIBLE].
And there were four boys.
And one of the girls that you see-- see here, up on the wall, Hannah [NON-ENGLISH].
And we just looked at each other.
And we had four to look at.
And they had two to look at.
And looked at Hannah in the eyes.
And she looked me in the eyes.
And I pointed to her in the book.
Because that's how it happened.
Our fate was stamped.
It was love at first sight.
And what they were discoursing and debating about right front of a bookstore that was full of Marxist literature.
We didn't know that the communist party was open-- opening in Egypt and in Cairo.
And they were-- they were debating about it.
And where she was, she was far, far, far, far beyond all the-- all the rest of them.
She-- she was a genius, a literary genius.
And she loved poems and so on.
You-- you know something about her, I-- I hope, and that I know you do.
No, no, no, no.
The--
--very first thing was we introduced each other.
I'm [HUNGARIAN] and I'm [HUNGARIAN], and I'm [HUNGARIAN].
[HUNGARIAN].
[LAUGHS]
Her name, yes.
Her-- her covert name.
And the-- and they are yes, I'm Josef.
And then we got friendly.
Well, we looked around.
Well, let's go sit down somewhere.
And we sat-- about half a block away there was an open-- opened terrace there, with [INAUDIBLE]--
No, no, no, no, no.
There were all the day before-- all the six of us.
And we sit in that beautiful sunset.
It was to us, well, it was, Christ, going from Canada, like this, into Cairo, well, what a change, huh?
And well, the strongest thing was orange juice.
But it was fresh orange juice.
And they will talk, talk, talk, talk.
But well, they saw we came from-- from Canada.
They came from-- from-- from Palestine.
Palestine just was a-- What kind of uniform did they have?
Oh, they had the same British uniform.
Had just a little bit of difference because the Canadian-- the Canadian uniform was-- was manufactured in Canada.
It was a better quality than what the British-- but exactly the same-- same style.
And they had in their-- well, the British Colonial Army.
So and they had the same-- same-- same-- same British-- British army uniform.
Did
No, no, no.
No, no, none of us.
None of us had rank.
But by the time we sat down around the table, we knew about each other, who the hell we are.
Because-- because of the secrecy, you see?
We noticed, oh, well, they just introduce selves and nothing else.
Just-- just the first name.
And well, that's how we were trained.
We never even tried to find-- find out-- well, who you are or what-- what you're doing.
We knew.
Oh, later-- later on.
Later on.
So then we had a couple drinks.
And then they invited with-- where we live, and-- they would-- well, that-- that much would-- well, we told them we live so and so far, name of the street.
We live in a-- in a private house.
And they told us well, that they issued-- they are in different quarters.
And so on.
But they had a club.
And they are 22 of them.
They gave us the address and told us they would draw a little map out.
Yeah, they're not very far from there.
And go and visit them.
So two-- two days later we came and-- and visited them.
It was-- it was a building, on a second floor.
On second floor, it was a large room there.
Very, very, very simple.
Had a long-- a long army table there.
And-- and benches to-- to sit around.
But they were waiting for us already.
And there too, there were only were orange juice to drink.
And then when I-- I was sitting here on this side.
And Adam is next to me, and all the rest around it.
And right across from there, there was another [INAUDIBLE].
And right beside him, it-- Hannah was sitting there.
And we just kept-- kept looking at each other.
But--
And then after-- after the few-- we exchanged about how we like Cairo, what-- what we had learned, coming so far away, land [NON-ENGLISH].
And when we told them we enjoy it very much, and we are overwhelmed with the-- with the tropical, et cetera, et cetera.
So idle talk, you see?

And all of a sudden, somebody started to sing in Hungarian, a rebel [HUNGARIAN] song that you could only sing underground or in isolated place where no [NON-ENGLISH]-- well, never about a [NON-ENGLISH], but nobody will-- [HUNGARIAN].
It's about Hungarian Red Army from the 1919, a song of the Red Army.
And he looked at Adam, and he looked back at-- at me, by Christ.
And when they came to the [HUNGARIAN], so we just got in with them.
And then they looked at [HUNGARIAN].
And there, [INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS]
So that's how it-- that's how it happened.
It was a test.
And they were prepared for it with-- we had to-- we have to test them out, if they-- they-- they-- they really are what we think they are.
And no, no, no questions.
That was enough.
And they sang the song right through.
Full-- with full--
With full-- [LAUGHS]
With full lung and so on.
And when we ended-- ended with the song, well-- well, we just melted together, you see?
And-- Did Hannah sing too?
Oh yeah.
Oh, yes, yes, yes.
And then somehow the-- well, we just-- then stood up and we went for another drink to-- to-- to the counter and so on.
And-- and somehow, just they came closer and closer.
And we just stepped closer.
There was a little balcony there.
The two of us.
But-- but after a few minutes, I could feel it, that she was an icicle.
No, she wasn't so easy.
No, no, no, no, no.
Took a couple days in there, when our hands touched first.
Yeah.
Well, probably the second or third-- third day.
Then slowly I-- I found that she was a really solid, determined-- determined girl.
And then-- then, as-- as we-- we went along-- we went along, then-- then I discovered he was-- in-- in some sense, she was too eager, too-- too anxious, and too-- and because of that, irresponsible.
Well, she was-- she was so, so eager to go, to go, to go.
And her-- her life, her-- her whole manner, always-- always, always disciplined, disciplined as soldier.
And then slowly I-- I begin to discovered-- well, you talked about Canada, and so on.
And I-- I told her that the whole story, of where I was, that we-- we-- we had-- we have Canada, we have Hungarian organization.
And indicated to her that-- that to the left.
Well, I was sure that I'm talking to the right-- right person, and that the Jewish organizations in-- in-- in Canada, same kind of leftist organization.
And then-- then she began to talk.
Oh see that-- felt it coming closer.

And so-- and then she began to-- she began to realize that, that I don't try to-- to boast or anything, but-- but she began to discover that the experience and the knowledge that I had-- after all, I was a co-editor of the newspaper and 13 years experience in the class struggle in Canada, and in the party where I could tell so much.
But-- but I just gave-- gave her in-- in small-- small doses.
And-- and then she-- she began to-- she began to ease up.
And then speaking about Canada, and that's-- that's in the book, when I spoke to her about Canada, told her about Canadian prairies and the beautiful wonders.
Well, we never-- we never asked that, not these kind of things, how or where you trained, what-- what have you learned and so on, or if you had partial training.
We never talked about the inside-- inside being the-- there was plenty-- plenty else to talk about.
Yeah, yeah.
No, no, no.
No, no.
She-- no, no.
This is-- she-- she didn't tell me about all of that.
The only-- only-- only very vague remarks here and there, as we went along.
Because the way you-- we just had to cover or cover up our past, you see?
In interrogation, you never-- and these things happen, tragic things happened.
And just a mistaken word in the interrogation when they caught you, and then they-- then you were finished.
Because they started to work on it.
And they beat it out-- out from you.
So what intimate things did you-- did you go through with her?
What kind of conversations, private conversations did you talk about?
Well, all-- all about-- all about the-- the beauties, natural beauties of the-- of the tropical land.
And so in our parties, and then we went for walks.
But it was always organized that an accidental meeting.
Oh, we're going to meet me here and there, but accidentally.
And usually if you were with somebody else and so on.
And then we separated.
You see, tried to make it as-- as natural as possible.
Because one-- that place was full of left behind-- not only German agents, but then we-- we discovered Arab-- Arab-- Arab spies who were spying for the Germans.
After all, the Germans were pretty close and left the-- the bastard behind.
Oh yes, well, we--
Well, we-- then-- then we came to that.
And that's-- that's in my book.
That then we to-- we went to the-- to theatres.

Or we went to-- went to resorts there, where the officers resort, of swimming pool and so on, and this kind-- we took-- took a long walk from that-- from that resort there, walking back to the-- back to the center.
That was the time where, then, where we really stopped, looked at each other, embraced, and well, my [INAUDIBLE], we were made to each other.
But took quite a while.
Yes.
Yes, we did say--
Well, just-- just in a [INAUDIBLE] as we could.
Because we were just the-- I mean, the-- the hell and whole assignment and our mission was ahead of us.
But when you are in that-- in that service, you never, never think about dying or anything.
You have your full confidence that am I going to pull through, go through with it.
But in the subconscious-- in the subconscious there is that, I never can-- never return from this kind of of hell.
And-- but-- but-- but then-- then we planned.
We had-- we had long journey down-- down on the Nile.
And the boss-- well, there was organized-- organized tours, you see.
And it was-- most of the time it was so-- and we visited the-- the Pyramids.
There I found out what kind of naughty-- really naughty and-- [LAUGHS] --dirty girl-- she-- she climbed on the top of the Great Pyramid.
I did too.
But she beat me to it.
Yeah.
And specially coming down, it was worse coming down.
Because of the-- of the gravitation.
You could feel it when you were up and when you were in the tower and just look out, it's a different thing.
But when you are in an open, up there, on an 800-feet-tall pyramid, on the top of it, and that was about that to that, square, on the top.
And from that-- the drape, they pulled-- pulled on the marble.
And they used it to build mosque with and so on, and in Cairo.
So you could-- you could climb it, because it was like-- like this, like steps, you see?
And she-- she climbed it.
[LAUGHS] On the next tape, I will ask you more questions about this period in Cairo.
